hot_topics.synthesis: As the United States approaches its 250th birthday, discussions are widespread about the nation's identity, history, and future. Articles reflect on enduring patriotism, the role of immigrants in the 'American Dream,' and the historical significance of events like the American Revolution. Despite challenges and waning faith in certain ideals, many pieces express hope and appreciation for the country's foundational principles.

Severe heatwaves are impacting both the United States and Europe, leading to significant concerns and even excess deaths in some regions like France. In the US, the heat threatens July 4th celebrations, while in Europe, forecasters warn of more extreme temperatures. Experts are advising simple hydration with water for most people to combat the heat.

Russia has launched a 'most massive' attack on Ukraine's capital, Kyiv, resulting in numerous fatalities and widespread destruction, described as retaliatory strikes. This latest escalation continues the conflict, even as reports suggest Russian advances may be slowing. The human cost of the war is also highlighted through personal stories of Ukrainian war widows.

hot_topics.topic: Challenges and Innovations in AI Development
hot_topics.synthesis: The field of Artificial Intelligence is experiencing both challenges and significant developments, including Meta CEO Mark Zuckerberg admitting that AI agent progress hasn't met expectations. Meanwhile, companies like Anthropic are exploring custom AI chips with partners such as Samsung, indicating a push for specialized hardware. The environmental cost of AI is also emerging as a concern, making it harder for tech giants to meet net-zero pledges.

items.title: Abyss
items.summary: Testimonies from Ukrainian war widows reveal profound insights into the mortal risks of love and the experience of a deep, living grief.

items.title: Artificial cell manages a few rounds of cell division
items.summary: Researchers at the University of Minnesota have developed a simplified artificial cell system that can enclose genetic material, continuously import new resources, and spontaneously divide for a few generations. This breakthrough offers new avenues for understanding the origin of life and minimalistic life forms.

items.title: Despite the darkness, I still see signs of hope in America
items.summary: The author reflects on America's 250th anniversary, recalling memories from the bicentennial and acknowledging the nation's past achievements like fighting fascism, the civil rights movement, and space exploration. Despite current challenges, the article expresses enduring hope for the country's future.

items.title: FAA proposal: Supersonic airliners can fly over US cities if they’re quiet
items.summary: The FAA has proposed overturning a 1973 ban on commercial supersonic flights over the US, allowing them if aircraft can significantly reduce sonic boom impacts on the ground. This proposal follows a Trump administration executive order and is based on technology like Boom Supersonic's Mach cutoff flights that refract shockwaves upwards.

items.title: Google loses long-running appeal of record EU fine, will have to cough up $4.7 billion
items.summary: Google has lost its final appeal in the European Union, confirming a $4.7 billion fine for abusing its Android monopoly by unfairly bundling search and Chrome as default apps. The Court of Justice of the European Union upheld the penalty, which was slightly reduced from its original 2018 amount.

items.title: Inside the Luddite festival harnessing Gen Z’s rage against Big Tech
items.summary: A 'Summer of Ludd' festival in New York City is drawing hundreds, including Gen Z, to engage in activities like offline dating and fighting data centers, all inspired by the historical Luddite movement's resistance to technology. The event features a play about the Luddites and aims to encourage community engagement away from digital devices.

items.title: Newly discovered PamStealer isn't your typical macOS malware
items.summary: Researchers have discovered PamStealer, a new macOS malware that uses a two-stage delivery system with AppleScript and a disk image to stealthily steal login credentials. It leverages the Pluggable Authentication Modules interface to validate passwords before sending them to attackers.

items.title: Plex debuts 5-year membership pass for $250
items.summary: Plex has introduced a new five-year membership pass for $250, while simultaneously increasing the price of its Lifetime Plex Pass to $750. This move reflects the company's financial goals and efforts to increase revenue, having previously offered lifetime access at much lower prices.

items.title: 'Most massive' Russian attack on Kyiv kills at least 30
items.summary: A "most massive" Russian attack on Kyiv has killed at least 30 people, deploying the largest number of weapons on the capital and hitting locations over a wide area. Russia described these as retaliatory attacks.

items.title: A mayor in Japan announced her maternity leave - and got the whole country talking
items.summary: Shoko Kawata, a 35-year-old mayor in Japan, sparked national discussion and criticism by announcing her maternity leave, but stated she loves her job and is proud to take time off for her baby. This highlights ongoing societal views on parental leave in Japan.

items.title: After 250 years the American Dream is surviving, but only just
items.summary: After 250 years, the American Dream continues to exist, but faith in this long-standing ideal is reportedly diminishing.

items.title: At least 32 dead after overcrowded bus plunges into ravine in Pakistan
items.summary: At least 32 people have died after an overcrowded bus traveling from Quetta to Peshawar crashed into a ravine in Pakistan's Dana Sar mountain range.

items.title: Bomb blast at Damascus cafe kills nine, Syrian state media say
items.summary: A bomb blast at a cafe near the Palace of Justice in Damascus, Syria, has killed nine people, according to Syrian state media, with no immediate claim of responsibility.

items.title: Ebola treatments trial begins in DR Congo
items.summary: A trial for new Ebola treatments has begun in the Democratic Republic of Congo, where there have been 1,406 confirmed cases, 301 suspected cases, and 438 deaths.

items.title: Former Olympian indicted for allegedly vandalising Washington Reflecting Pool
items.summary: A former Olympian has been indicted on charges of property destruction for allegedly vandalizing the Washington Reflecting Pool, following renovations championed by President Donald Trump.

items.title: France records 2,025 excess deaths at peak of heatwave as Europe braces for more extreme weather
items.summary: France experienced 2,025 excess deaths during the peak of a recent heatwave, while forecasters warn of further extreme temperatures across Europe.

items.title: German row over plan for workers to need sick note on first day of illness
items.summary: A proposed German rule requiring workers to obtain a sick note on the first day of illness has sparked controversy, with a doctors' group calling it "madness" due to the burden it places on patients.

items.title: Instagram running ads promoting child sexual abuse material in India, BBC finds
items.summary: The BBC has found that Instagram is running advertisements in India that promote child sexual abuse material, using terms like "rape" and "child video" to link to content on Telegram.

items.title: Iran begins public mourning for Ayatollah killed in February
items.summary: Iran has commenced public mourning ceremonies for an Ayatollah who was killed in February, with his body set to lie in state in Tehran's Grand Mosalla ahead of multi-day funeral events.

items.title: Killers of British couple in South Africa sentenced to life
items.summary: The killers of a British couple, who were collecting rare plants and seeds before their bodies were found in a river, have been sentenced to life in South Africa.

items.title: On the Strait of Hormuz, BBC finds seized ships and shark fishermen as uneasy calm returns
items.summary: The BBC has reported from the Iranian side of the Strait of Hormuz, detailing findings of seized ships and the activities of shark fishermen, indicating an uneasy calm in the region. This marks the first time journalists from a UK broadcaster have visited the area.

items.title: Ukrainian suspect hunted by police after Monaco bomb attack was 'disguised as a man'
items.summary: Police are searching for a Ukrainian suspect, believed to have been disguised as a man, in connection with a bomb attack in Monaco, with officials suggesting she may not have acted alone. Investigations indicate the suspect spent days casing out the scene.

items.title: US withdraws troops from Nigeria after Islamic State mission
items.summary: The US has withdrawn its troops from Nigeria following a successful joint operation against Islamic State, which began in December of the previous year.

items.title: Vatican excommunicates followers of global Catholic sect
items.summary: The Vatican has excommunicated approximately 600,000 followers of the Society of Saint Pius X, a traditionalist Catholic sect, affecting a significant portion of its global adherents.

items.title: Zuma showing South Africa 'middle finger' by meeting Gupta brother - minister
items.summary: A South African minister accused former President Jacob Zuma of disrespecting the nation by meeting a Gupta brother, reigniting anger over a decade-old corruption scandal. Zuma's trip to India sparked the controversy.

items.title: Alibaba to ban Claude Code in workplace over alleged backdoor risks, source says
items.summary: Alibaba is reportedly planning to ban the use of Claude Code within its workplace due to alleged backdoor risks associated with the software.

items.title: CarPlay Is Additive
items.summary: The article argues that CarPlay serves as an additive feature, enhancing the in-car experience rather than replacing core functions, despite differing opinions.

items.title: crustc: entirety of `rustc`, translated to C
items.summary: "crustc" is a project that translates the entirety of the Rust compiler (`rustc`) into C, offering an alternative implementation or a deep dive into compiler architecture.

items.title: Exapunks (2018)
items.summary: This refers to "Exapunks," a hacking-themed puzzle game released in 2018 by Zachtronics, known for its challenging programming puzzles.

items.title: Half-Baked Product
items.summary: This article discusses the issues and implications of releasing a "half-baked product" to the market.

items.title: Immich 3.0
items.summary: Immich 3.0 has been released, bringing significant updates to the self-hosted photo and video backup solution.

items.title: Podman v6.0.0
items.summary: Podman v6.0.0 has been released, introducing new features and improvements to the container management tool.

items.title: Right to Local Intelligence
items.summary: The "Right to Local Intelligence" initiative advocates for the ability to process data and run AI models locally, promoting privacy and user control over their information.

items.title: Since Linux 6.9, LUKS suspend stopped wiping disk-encryption keys from memory
items.summary: A change in Linux kernel 6.9 has resulted in LUKS suspend no longer wiping disk-encryption keys from memory, which could have security implications.

items.title: The Safari MCP server for web developers
items.summary: WebKit has introduced the Safari MCP (Multi-platform Content Protection) server, a new tool for web developers.

items.title: Zuckerberg 'Admits' Meta's Layoffs Were Ineffective
items.summary: An article reports that Mark Zuckerberg has admitted Meta's recent layoffs were ineffective, suggesting the company's workforce reduction strategy did not yield desired results.

items.title: A hot summer trend in the sharing economy? Rental swimming pools
items.summary: Rental swimming pools are emerging as a popular summer trend in the sharing economy, with company Swimply reporting approximately 275,000 private pool reservations this year.

items.title: But first, coffee: The drink that energized the American Revolution
items.summary: Coffee played a significant role in the American Revolution, as colonial Americans consumed it before the Boston Tea Party, and coffee-serving establishments served as hubs for revolutionary ideas.

items.title: Former USAID head grieves its closure while hoping for its future
items.summary: A former USAID administrator reflects on the agency's closure a year ago and expresses hope for the future direction of US foreign aid.

items.title: Hakeem Jeffries addresses Democratic Party divisions, says Trump is the bigger issue
items.summary: House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries discussed Democratic Party divisions, stating that Donald Trump poses a greater concern on the eve of America's 250th birthday. He addressed the party's midterm future with NPR.

items.title: How a fertilizer shortage caused by the Iran war could affect U.S. food prices
items.summary: The ongoing Iran war has disrupted fertilizer and natural gas shipments through the Strait of Hormuz, but a major increase in US grocery food prices is deemed unlikely despite the shortage.

items.title: How young people feel about American identity, on the nation's 250th birthday
items.summary: This report explores how young people perceive American identity and the concepts of "life, liberty and the pursuit of happiness" as the nation approaches its 250th birthday.

items.title: July 4th events threatened by heat wave. And, Russia strikes on Ukraine's capital
items.summary: July 4th celebrations in the US are endangered by a severe heat wave, while Russia has launched attacks on Ukraine's capital, resulting in fatalities, which it claims are retaliatory.

items.title: Just gave birth or are about to? Share your story and questions about costs
items.summary: NPR is inviting individuals who have recently given birth or are expecting to share their experiences and questions regarding the often unpredictable costs of having a baby in the US healthcare system.

items.title: Morning news brief
items.summary: This news brief covers threats to US 250th celebrations due to heat, Russia's slowed advances with Ukraine hitting back, and internal Democratic infighting risking House hopes.

items.title: Oregon ER doctors win a 'David and Goliath' battle against a national company
items.summary: Emergency room doctors in Eugene, Oregon, successfully fended off a national physician staffing firm attempting to replace them, marking a significant victory under a new state law. Their case is attracting national attention as other states consider similar legislation to counter corporate medicine.

items.title: It's coming home ... in the wee hours
items.summary: British Prime Minister Keir Starmer will pass a last-minute law allowing pubs to remain open until 5 a.m. for England's next World Cup match, easing licensing rules despite some local council resistance. This ensures fans can watch the early-morning game in pubs.

items.title: Spot the pol!
items.summary: FBI Director Kash Patel was spotted chatting with FIFA President Gianni Infantino and Secretary of State Marco Rubio at a World Cup match, highlighting political figures' presence at major sporting events.

items.title: The Croatian team’s favorite singer is a fascist salute away from the mainstream
items.summary: Marko Perković, known as Thompson, is Croatia's most popular singer and the unofficial anthem performer for the national football team, despite his music and performances featuring Ustashe regime fascist salutes and themes. His popularity highlights Croatia's ongoing struggle to reckon with its World War II fascist past.

items.title: Understand to participate
items.summary: Geoffrey Litt's "Understand to participate" concept, discussed at AIE, emphasizes the need for humans to deeply understand code generated by AI coding agents to collaborate effectively and avoid cognitive debt. This fluency allows for active participation in the creative process rather than passively accepting agent outputs.

items.title: Using DSPy to evaluate and improve Datasette Agent's SQL system prompts
items.summary: Simon Willison used DSPy to evaluate and improve the system prompts for Datasette Agent's SQL query feature, identifying issues like column-name guessing due to insufficient schema information. The evaluation, performed with Claude Fable 5, suggested improvements such as including column names in the schema listing.

items.title: A warning sign about AI’s real cost, courtesy of Google and Amazon
items.summary: The increasing demands of artificial intelligence are making it significantly more challenging for major tech companies like Amazon and Google to meet their net-zero emissions commitments. This indicates a hidden environmental cost associated with AI development and usage.

items.title: Anthropic is discussing a new custom chip with Samsung
items.summary: Anthropic is reportedly in discussions with Samsung regarding the development of a new custom AI chip, following a similar move by OpenAI which partnered with Broadcom.

items.title: Boeing-owned Wisk Aero accused of firing manager who raised safety concerns
items.summary: A former software manager at Wisk Aero, a Boeing-owned company, alleges he was fired for raising safety concerns about rushed software testing ahead of a critical 2025 flight test.

items.title: IQM, Europe’s first public quantum company, admits the future of the tech is uncertain
items.summary: IQM, a quantum computing company from Finland, has gone public on Nasdaq with a valuation of $1.9 billion, but its CEO admits that the future of quantum technology remains uncertain.

items.title: Jersey Mike’s IPO illustrates how bad the AI hype has become
items.summary: The IPO documents for sandwich shop Jersey Mike's unexpectedly include mentions of AI, highlighting the extent of current AI hype even in unrelated industries.

items.title: Mark Zuckerberg tells staff that AI agents haven’t progressed as quickly as he’d hoped
items.summary: At an internal meeting, Meta CEO Mark Zuckerberg reportedly told staff that the company's AI development efforts, particularly concerning AI agents, are not advancing as quickly as he had expected.

items.title: Politician who investigated spyware abuses had his phone hacked with Pegasus spyware
items.summary: A European politician, who was serving on an EU committee investigating the spyware industry, had their phone hacked with NSO Group's Pegasus spyware. The attack was carried out by a government customer of NSO Group.

items.title: Thiel Capital’s Jack Selby nabs stakes in hot startups like Etched through Arizona connections
items.summary: Jack Selby of Thiel Capital has acquired stakes in promising startups like Etched through his Arizona connections. His VC firm, Copper Sky Capital, is reportedly raising a $300 million second fund.

items.title: Travel app Hopper to pay $35M in FTC settlement over ‘unfairly’ charging hidden fees
items.summary: Travel app Hopper will pay $35 million to settle allegations from the FTC that it used deceptive "dark patterns" to conceal fees and mislead users about service costs and benefits.

items.title: Caitlin Flanagan: The Indivisible America
items.summary: Caitlin Flanagan reflects on learning the Pledge of Allegiance in Berkeley, California, in 1968, and how it instilled in her a deep sense of patriotism and respect for the American flag. She asserts that America remains steady despite challenges, and an American passport is invaluable.

items.title: Nikki Haley: We Are Blessed to Live in America
items.summary: Nikki Haley reflects on America's 250th birthday, honoring her immigrant parents' journey from India to the US to find freedom and opportunity despite discrimination. She emphasizes that Calvin Coolidge's speech on the 150th anniversary echoes the enduring principles of hard work and freedom her parents instilled.

items.title: Rod Dreher: My American Homecoming
items.summary: Rod Dreher shares his experience returning to America after four years in Budapest, noting the shock of living costs but also rediscovering appreciation for American culture, such as magnolias and dive bars in the South. He finds there's no place he'd rather be despite the country's chaos and high prices.

items.title: The American Revolution Was a World War, with Richard Bell
items.summary: Historian Richard Bell discusses how the American Revolution evolved into a global conflict, extending beyond North America to the Caribbean, Europe, and India. He explores George Washington's strategy that forced Britain to engage in a worldwide war.

items.title: The Greatest Country on Earth
items.summary: The Free Press dedicates its newsletter to America's 250th birthday, reflecting on the enduring foundation of democracy laid by the Founding Fathers and celebrating American values. Various writers contribute essays on their love for the country, including stories of patriotism, immigrant experiences, and historical figures like Henry Kissinger.

items.title: A behind-the-scenes look at Midjourney’s medical scanner leaves many questions unanswered
items.summary: Midjourney has released a behind-the-scenes video of its "dunk-tank" ultrasound medical scanner, which it hopes will revolutionize medicine with cheap, radiation-free imaging in spas. Despite the video, the AI startup has yet to provide substantial proof that the futuristic scanner actually works as claimed.

items.title: Apple TV is hitting its stride
items.summary: Apple TV is gaining a reputation for quality over quantity in its content, with 2026 marking a particularly strong year for new hits and returning favorites across genres. This strategy has led to comparisons with the early days of HBO, indicating the service is finding its footing.

items.title: Hydration isn’t complicated: Just drink water
items.summary: As extreme heat waves affect various regions, this article from 'Optimizer' emphasizes that simple water is generally sufficient for hydration, rather than electrolyte drinks, unless one is engaged in intense physical activity. It encourages focusing on basic hydration despite ongoing debates about climate and health.

items.title: I finally got my Trump phone
items.summary: The Verge staff have finally received their Trump Mobile T1 Phone 8002s, over a year after ordering them, noting unconventional delivery and inclusions like a free SIM card. Despite the wait and issues, the phones arrived, with three units received despite only two being ordered and paid for.

items.title: Jon Prosser responds to Apple lawsuit by blaming the other guy
items.summary: YouTuber Jon Prosser has denied conspiring to injure Apple in response to a lawsuit regarding stolen iOS secrets, but admitted to recording and sharing a FaceTime call showing unreleased iOS software. He also acknowledged sharing revenue from leak-related YouTube videos and claimed another defendant was responsible for the disclosure.

items.title: Sony’s PlayStation disc factory is already being repurposed
items.summary: Sony is repurposing its main PlayStation disc manufacturing plant in Thalgau, Austria, to produce optical microlenses as demand for video game discs declines significantly. The facility, which currently produces 600,000 discs daily, will retrain its 300 employees for the new manufacturing focus by 2028.

items.title: This slim camera has a transparent LCD screen for a viewfinder
items.summary: Godox has joined the trend of simple point-and-shoot cameras with its new C100, which features a unique transparent LCD screen that functions as both a viewfinder and display. This design aims to differentiate it from other popular compact cameras and smartphone shooters.
